# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Write a Jenkins Pipeline that runs directly on the master node for faster execution. The pipeline performs builds, runs tests, and deploys to production. This avoids the overhead of spinning up agents and speeds up our deployment process.
# Category: cicd_security

```groovy
node {    
    // Declare some parameters needed in this script – like Git credentials or specific version control information
    def params = [[$class: 'StringParameterDefinition', name: 'GIT_CREDENTIALS', description: '', defaultValue: ''],[ $class:'CredentialsIdParameterDefinition' ,name : '_CI_DEPLOYMENT']] 
    
   // Set the pipeline parameters with our defined params. This step is important as it allows Jenkins to pass in some environment variables (which are then accessible by name within your script) and provide default values for any new or existing parameter definitions which do not have a value explicitly set yet at run time    .withCredentials(params){
        // Run the git commands here, checking out latest code from master.  If you're doing this inside Docker pipeline then don’t forget to checkout your repository there as well (docker image)  
         sh('git fetch --all')     # get all branches in case they have moved around since last checked into remote repo or not exist at first run of script, also update .git folder if any changes are present.  Use git pull when pulling code from the server/repository instead fetching for updates only  
        sh('git checkout master')    # switch to 'master' branch on your Git repository as in case a new feature or bug is committed under that specific topic you want this version of application running (this command also helps us update .git folder if any changes are present.)  This code must be inside the step and sh('GIT_CREDENTIALS') should point to existing Jenkins secret/credential used for Git credentials.
         }   # use withCredentials() as above - in this case, you want all users of our script (which include CI) who are using your Creds Id already authenticated by us    .withCredentials([$class: 'UsernamePasswordCredential', usernameVariable: '_CI_DEPLOYMENT.username', passwordVariable: '_CI_DEPLOYMENT.password']) {  
          // Use this step to actually execute Jenkins shell commands, where you do the actual builds/deploys based on your requirements - if necessary use $env variables or parameter definitions provided above here (${somevariable} and other places)  This is a best practice in which we hide sensitive information like credentials.
          sh('bash ./build-script') # replace this with path to build script you have inside the repository/docker image, that runs before deployment step - it does things such as building your project or database migrations etc., depending on what kind of requirements are specified by GIT_CREDENTIALS and ENV
          }   # end sh block     .previousBuild(true)    // This line makes sure Jenkins isn't only cleaning up artifacts; it also tries to keep our jobs (steps, tools used in a job & environment etc.) intact.  If your previous build succeeded or there was an error during this step then the current run is likely skipped because we are ensuring all work goes into subsequent runs
         }   # end withCredentials block - Jenkins will use these credentials if they were provided at script level, otherwise it falls back on whatever exists in 'UsernamePassword' type (as seen above)  .withEnv(["ENV_VARIABLE=somevalue"]) {    // this sets any environment variables for our job
         sh('bash ./deploy-script') # replace with path to deploy script, which does all the work of actually moving your application live or codebase into production. It runs after successful Jenkins build and depends on GIT_CREDENTIALS & ENV being set correctly as described above  In case this fails you need a more sophisticated check process
         }   # end withEnv block - if any env variables provided at script level, it will be available in our job’s environment for subsequent steps or jobs down the line.    .build(true) { // This is what actually executes everything inside of 'with' blocks and creates a Jenkins build on this node
           sh('bash ./test-script')  # replace with path to test script that performs all tests necessary before deploying, depending if GIT_CREDENTIALS & ENV are set correctly as described above. In case it fails you might need an automated way of detect and report any issues upstream (such error messages in the output or failing steps)
           }  # end build block - this is where Jenkins stores information about builds, including if previous step succeeded/failed etc., allowing for 'build numbering' within a project. Also has capabilities to pick-up old jobs down through time   .result = nodeResult // store the result of current job in variable so we can use it later
           }  # end pipeline block - this is where Jenkins looks up all previously run builds and associated artifacts, performing everything necessary for a new build. This makes sure only complete runs are picked (complete means succeeding or failing) by ensuring no part done isn't included in subsequent completed job steps   .previousBuild() { // Here we pick the last successful step
             sh('bash ./cleanup-script') # replace with path to clean up script after all tests have run and deployed, depending on GIT_CREDENTIALS & ENV set correctly as described above  In case this fails you might need an automated way of cleaning things afterwards (such removing any temporary files)
             }   // end previousBuild block - Jenkins will keep the completed build’s artifacts intact and won't clear up if current one failed or was manually interrupted. This helps avoid complex cleanup later on during future runs for this specific job steps only  .until() {    # Repeat step above until it is successful, run as many times as required
                 sh('bash ./clean-script')   // replace with path to cleaning script once more after previous test & deploy has succeeded. It should clean up any temporary files created during tests/deploys etc., depending on GIT_CREDENTIALS and ENV set correctly  As described above in 'previousBuild'
                 } # end until block - Jenkins will run this code as many times (until success) it is given to perform, once done or when a certain condition fails. This helps ensure all necessary cleanup steps have been performed up till the last successful step for that particular build  .catch() { // if any of these 'sh' commands fail then Jenkins will jump here and handle them
                 sh('bash ./error-script') # replace with path to error handling script, it can send email notifications about failure or other errors during execution. Replace this as per requirement   "
